<pre class="source-text">   ECO10005-Economics for Business
   Decision Making

   Aggregate demand and aggregate supply



    Professor Abbas Valadkhani

    Week 9











1




              Aggregate demand and
             aggregate supply model
      A model that explains short-run fluctuations in real
       GDP and the price level.

      Similarities between micro and macro in that:

        − We need to understand movement along curve and shift in
          curve;

        − Real GDP and the price level are determined in the short
          run by the intersection of the aggregate demand curve and
          the short-run aggregate supply curve.





     2


2




                                                                                                           1

                THE AD/AS MODEL



      The model provides us with an important tool for
       analysing relationships between real GDP,
       employment (and unemployment) and the price
       level.


        –we begin with Aggregate Demand







3




           AGGREGATE  DEMAND


      Aggregate Demand represents the quantity of goods
       and services that consumers, businesses,
       governments and foreign buyers are willing and able
       to buy at various price levels ruling in the economy.

      Total planned expenditure on output is therefore
       equal to the sum of:

                C + I + G + X –M




4

6




                                                                                   3

                      Aggregate demand


      Aggregate demand (AD) curve: A curve that
       shows the relationship between the price level, the
       quantity of real GDP demanded by households,
       firms and the government, plus net exports.

      Short-run aggregate supply (                  SRAS    ) curve: A
       curve that shows the relationship in the short run
       between the price level and the quantity of real GDP
       supplied.










     7


7








      MAIN CHARACTERISTICS OF THE THREE SEGMENTS

    Keynesian segment         – there is significant underutilization of
    resources with high unemployment and lots of spare plant capacity.
    Output can expand without the need for higher prices. Associated
    with an economy in depression –1930’s.

    Intermediate segment          – resources are more heavily utilized; but
    business costs rise more slowly as prices rise, so profits rise and
    firms are willing to increase supply.  Higher output is associated
    with rising prices.

    Classical segment       – resources are fullyutilized. Output cannot
    increase but prices will rise sharply when there is an expansion of
    AD.

     B        Intermediate Section –Upward sloping Supply Curve
              As AD changes so do prices. Tend to concentrate on this section.

     C        Classical / Vertical Section –economy at full capacity
              Increase in AD –no change in real GDP, large rise in price.

11




         Why is the aggregate demand
               curve downward sloping?
    1.     The wealth effect
           –    Increase in price level reduces wealth (espcash)
                which in turn reduces consumption.
    2.     The interest-rate effect
          −    Increase in price level increases need for cash/funds
               which increases interest rates and decreases C and I
    3.     The international-trade effect
           –    Increase in the price level (relative to trading partners)
                exports become more expensive and imports cheaper
                net exports. Exports fall and imports rise.




     12
      Inverse relationship between real GDP and prices

12




                                                                                                                               6

   Variables that shift the aggregate
                          demand curve

    1.    Changes in government policies.
           –  Examples: Taxes, government purchases.
    2.    Changes in the official interest rate set by the CB (RBA)                .


    3.    Changes in the expectations of households and firms.
           –  Rise in confidence: investment and consumption increase
    4.    Changes in foreign variables.



     13    –  Examples: Exchange rates, relative income levels between
              countries.

13




        Short-run Aggregate supply


    The short-run aggregate supply (              SRAS) curve

      The SRAS       is upward sloping, showing that, in the short run,
       firms will produce more in response to higher prices.
      The prices of inputs tends to rise more slowly than the
       prices of final products.

          –   Contracts make some wages and prices ‘sticky’.
          –   Firms are often slow to adjust wages.
          –   Menu costs make some prices sticky.               Menu costs       are
              costs to firms of changing prices.


     14


14




                                                                                                                  7

              What shifts the SRAS curve

     Variables that shift the                      SRAS        curve


     1.     Expected changes in the future price level.

     2.     Adjustments of workers and firms to errors in past
            expectations about the price level.

     3.     Unexpected changes in the price of an important
            natural resource (e.gthe price of crude oil).

     4.     Natural disasters.

                                                                                                                                            8

       Long-Run Aggregate Supply


    Long-run aggregate supply (                LRAS) curve: A
     curve that shows the relationship in the long run
     between the price level and the quantity of real GDP
     supplied.

    The long-run aggregate supply curve shows that, in
     the long run, increases in the price level do not
     affect the level of real GDP.

    The long-run aggregate supply curve is a vertical
     line at potential GDP.


    17


17




    Shifts in the long-run aggregate
                        supply curve

    The LRAS     curve shifts because potential GDP increases
     over time.
    Increases in potential GDP (or economic growth) are due to:


       1.   An increase in resources.
       2.   An increase in machinery and equipment.
       3.   New technology.







    18


18

                             Aggregate supply

     Variables that shift the short-run and long-run aggregate
     supply curves

       1.    Increases in the labour force and/or in the capital stock,
             and/or in resources.

       2.    Technological change.

20

       Macroeconomic Equilibrium in
           the Long Run and the Short
                                         Run


       In long-run equilibrium, the aggregate demand and short-
        run aggregate supply curves intersect at a point along the
        long-run aggregate supply curve.

        Macroeconomic equilibrium in

     Expansion

     1.     The short-run effect of an increase in aggregate
            demand.
            –     AD     curve shifts right, and real GDP and the
                  price level rise.

     2.     Adjustment back to potential GDP in the long run.
            –     Automatic adjustment mechanism                                : SRAS
                  curve shifts left, (which may take a year or
                  more).

                COMPARING DEMAND-PULL AND COST-PUSH
                                                  INFLATION
      Recall some earlier examples of factors that will cause
        shifts in short-run aggregate supply!

      Higher production costs that result from higher oil prices,
        for example, will shift SRAS leftwards. This will lead to
        higher prices and lower real GDP.

      The result will be cost-push inflation and it gives rise to an
        economic outcome called stagflation                                     –higher inflation
        and higher unemployment occurring simultaneously.

29




                ECONOMIC POLICIES


        Policies to tackle inflation and unemployment:
          –Demand-side policies
              •fiscal policy
              •monetary policy
          –Supply-side policies. Examples include the significant microeconomic
            reforms in Australia during the 1980s and 1990s
          –Incomes policy. This was very important in the 1980s under the Hawke
            Labour Government.


        There are differing views among some economists about the relative
        effectiveness of fiscal and monetary policy.
